strands.name: Number & Place Value
strands.description: Reading, writing, comparing and rounding numbers.

teach.title: Place value
teach.plain: Place value tells you how much each digit is worth. The same digit means something different depending on where it sits.
rules: Reading right to left: ones, tens, hundreds, thousands, ten thousands.
A 4 in the ones column is worth 4. A 4 in the hundreds column is worth 400.
Zeros are placeholders. They keep other digits in the right column.
teach.example: In 3,472 the 4 is in the hundreds column, so it means 400. The 3 is in the thousands column, so it means 3,000.
teach.tryThis: What is the value of the 7 in 8,741?

workedExample.question: Write the value of each digit in 5,283.
steps: Start at the right-hand digit: 3 is in the ones column, so it means 3.
Next is 8, in the tens column, so it means 80.
Next is 2, in the hundreds column, so it means 200.
Last is 5, in the thousands column, so it means 5,000.

practice.question: What is the value of the 6 in 4,632?
practice.workingOut: The 6 sits in the hundreds column, so it is worth 600.

practice.question: What is the value of the 9 in 9,214?
practice.workingOut: The 9 is in the thousands column, so it is worth 9,000.

practice.question: Which number has a 5 worth 50?
practice.workingOut: In 2,051 the 5 is in the tens column, so it means 50.

practice.question: In 7,408, what is the value of the 4?
practice.workingOut: The 4 sits in the hundreds column, so it is worth 400.

practice.question: Which number is made from 3,000 + 600 + 20 + 5?
What is the value of the 0 in 5,031?
practice.workingOut: The 0 is a placeholder in the hundreds column. It holds the column but is worth nothing.

teach.title: Comparing numbers
teach.plain: To compare two numbers, look at the digits from the left. The first digit that is different tells you which number is bigger.
rules: First, count the digits. More digits = bigger number.
If both numbers have the same number of digits, compare from the left.
Use < for 'less than' and > for 'greater than'. The open mouth points to the bigger number.
teach.example: Compare 4,523 and 4,498. Same digits (4). Next: 5 is bigger than 4. So 4,523 > 4,498.
teach.tryAnswer: 3,270 (hundreds digit 2 vs tens digit 0, the hundreds differ: 2 > 0)

workedExample.question: Put these numbers in order, smallest first: 2,341, 2,314, 2,431, 2,143
steps: Compare hundreds: 2,143 has 1; the other three have 3 or 4.
Of the others, 2,314 and 2,341 have hundreds digit 3; 2,431 has 4, so it is largest.

practice.question: Which symbol goes in the box? 4,523 □ 4,532
practice.workingOut: Tens: 2 < 3. So 4,523 is less than 4,532.

practice.question: Which is the largest?
practice.workingOut: Hundreds digits: 4, 4, 9, 0. Largest hundreds is 9. So 3,904.

practice.question: Which is the smallest?
practice.workingOut: All start 1. Hundreds: 2, 3, 2, 4. Two start 1,2. Tens: 3 vs 4. 1,234 is smallest.

practice.question: Order smallest first: 567, 657, 576. Which is in the middle?
practice.workingOut: In order: 567, 576, 657. The middle one is 576.

practice.question: Which statement is true?
practice.workingOut: 1,100 is bigger because the hundreds digit is 1 vs 0.

practice.question: Which is the largest?
practice.workingOut: Hundreds: 0, 8, 7, 0. 8 is biggest, so 9,807.

teach.title: Rounding numbers
teach.plain: Rounding means making a number simpler. We round to the nearest 10, 100 or 1,000 so numbers are easier to work with.
rules: Find the digit to the RIGHT of the place you are rounding to.
If it is 5 or more, round UP.
If it is 4 or less, round DOWN.
All digits after the rounded place become zero.
teach.example: Round 347 to the nearest 100. Look at the tens digit (4). 4 is less than 5, so round DOWN to 300.

workedExample.question: Round 4,650 to the nearest 1,000.
steps: We are rounding to the nearest 1,000, so look at the hundreds digit.
6 is 5 or more, so round UP.
The thousands digit 4 becomes 5, and all digits after become zero.

practice.question: Round 67 to the nearest 10.
practice.workingOut: Ones digit is 7, which is 5 or more. Round up to 70.

practice.question: Round 432 to the nearest 100.
practice.workingOut: Tens digit is 3, less than 5. Round down to 400.

practice.question: Round 2,750 to the nearest 1,000.
practice.workingOut: Hundreds digit is 7, so round up to 3,000.

practice.question: Round 849 to the nearest 10.
practice.workingOut: Ones digit is 9, so round up to 850.

practice.question: Round 6,320 to the nearest 100.
practice.workingOut: Tens digit is 2, less than 5. Round down to 6,300.

practice.question: Round 9,499 to the nearest 1,000.
practice.workingOut: Hundreds digit is 4, less than 5. Round down to 9,000.

practice.question: Round 5,551 to the nearest 100.
practice.workingOut: For nearest 100, look at the tens digit. Tens digit is 5, so round up to 5,600.

teach.title: Negative numbers
teach.plain: Negative numbers are less than zero. We see them in temperatures, money owed and going below ground floor.
rules: Numbers get bigger going right on a number line.
-5 is SMALLER than -2 (further from zero).
To go from a negative to a positive, count up through zero.
The difference between two temperatures is how many steps between them.
teach.example: The temperature was 4°C, then dropped by 7°C. 4 - 7 = -3°C. Count back from 4 past zero: 4, 3, 2, 1, 0, -1, -2, -3.
teach.tryThis: The temperature is -2°C. It rises by 5°C. What is the new temperature?

workedExample.question: The temperature at midnight was 3°C. By 5am it had dropped by 8°C. What was the new temperature?
steps: We need to go DOWN by 8.
3 - 8 means go back 8 steps on the number line.
3 → 0 is 3 steps. We still need 5 more steps BELOW zero.

practice.question: 11°C drops by 17°C. What is the new temperature?
practice.workingOut: 11 - 17 = -6°C. Go back 11 to reach 0, then 6 more below zero.

practice.question: What is the difference between -3°C and 6°C?
practice.workingOut: From -3 to 0 is 3 steps. From 0 to 6 is 6 steps. Total: 9°C.

practice.question: Which is colder?
practice.workingOut: -7 is further below zero, so it is colder.

practice.question: Order from smallest: -4, 2, -1, 0, 3. Which is smallest?
practice.workingOut: -4 is furthest below zero, so it is smallest.

practice.question: The temperature is -5°C. It rises by 9°C. What is it now?
practice.workingOut: -5 + 9: go up 5 to 0, then 4 more to 4°C.

strands.name: Four Operations
strands.description: Adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing with bigger numbers.

teach.title: Column addition
teach.plain: Column addition lines up the digits under each other so you can add one column at a time.
rules: Line up the ones, tens, hundreds and thousands columns.
Start adding from the RIGHT (ones first).
If a column adds to 10 or more, carry the tens digit to the next column.
Write the carried digit small under the next column.
teach.example: 3,456 + 2,789. Ones: 6+9=15, write 5, carry 1. Tens: 5+8+1=14, write 4, carry 1. Hundreds: 4+7+1=12, write 2, carry 1. Thousands: 3+2+1=6. Answer: 6,245.

workedExample.question: Work out 15,678 + 9,432 using column addition.
steps: Line up: 15,678 above 9,432 (line up the ones).

practice.workingOut: Line up and add: ones 6+9=15 carry 1; tens 5+8+1=14 carry 1; hundreds 4+7+1=12 carry 1; thousands 3+2+1=6. Answer 6,245.

practice.question: Year 5 has 156 pupils and Year 6 has 143. How many altogether?
practice.workingOut: 156 + 143. Ones 6+3=9; tens 5+4=9; hundreds 1+1=2. Answer 299 pupils.

practice.question: A library has 3,456 fiction and 2,789 non-fiction books. How many books in total?

practice.workingOut: 78+22=100 (easy pair first). Then 100+145=245.

practice.question: A cinema sold 1,234 on Friday, 1,567 on Saturday and 1,098 on Sunday. Total tickets?

teach.title: Column subtraction
teach.plain: Column subtraction lines up the digits so you can take away one column at a time. When you can't take a bigger digit from a smaller one, you borrow from the next column.
rules: Line up the columns (ones under ones, tens under tens).
Start from the RIGHT (ones first).
If the top digit is smaller than the bottom, borrow 1 from the next column.
Borrowing from tens means the top ones digit gets 10 extra.
teach.example: 52 - 38. Ones: 2 - 8 can't do. Borrow 1 from tens: 12 - 8 = 4. Tens: 4 - 3 = 1. Answer: 14.

workedExample.question: Work out 8,234 - 3,567.
steps: Ones: 4 - 7 can't do. Borrow from tens. 3 becomes 2, ones becomes 14. 14 - 7 = 7.
Tens: 2 - 6 can't do. Borrow from hundreds. 2 becomes 1, tens becomes 12. 12 - 6 = 6.
Hundreds: 1 - 5 can't do. Borrow from thousands. 8 becomes 7, hundreds becomes 11. 11 - 5 = 6.

practice.workingOut: Borrow: ones 16-9=7; tens 4-8 borrow → 14-8=6; hundreds 0. Answer 67.
Borrow across zeros. 1,000 - 347 = 653.

practice.question: A shop had 5,000 footballs. They sold 2,847. How many are left?
A farmer had 8,000 apples. He sold 3,245. How many left?
The museum had 1,567 visitors on Tuesday and 1,234 on Monday. How many MORE on Tuesday?
School needs £5,000. They have £2,750. How much more to raise?

teach.title: Times tables
teach.plain: Times tables are the building blocks of multiplication. Knowing them by heart makes every other bit of maths quicker.
rules: Multiplying is repeated adding. 4 × 3 means 4 + 4 + 4.
Order doesn't matter: 3 × 4 = 4 × 3.
To multiply by 10, add a zero to the end.
To multiply by 100, add two zeros.

workedExample.question: Work out 6 × 8 step by step.
steps: 6 × 8 is 'six lots of eight' OR 'eight lots of six' — same answer.

practice.workingOut: 25 × 4 = 100 (like four 25p coins = £1).

teach.title: Long multiplication
teach.plain: When a number is too big for your times tables, you can split it up, multiply each part, and add the answers together.
rules: Write the bigger number on top.
Multiply the top number by the ONES of the bottom.
Multiply the top number by the TENS of the bottom (remember to put a 0 in the ones place).
Add the two answers together.

workedExample.question: Work out 4,289 × 24.
steps: Step 2: Multiply 4,289 by 20 (the tens). First do 4,289 × 2 = 8,578, then add a zero: 85,780.

practice.question: A school orders 36 boxes of pencils with 24 in each. How many pencils?
practice.workingOut: 36 × 24 = 864 pencils.

teach.title: Division
teach.plain: Dividing means sharing equally or grouping. 20 ÷ 4 means 'how many fours fit into 20?'
rules: Division is the OPPOSITE of multiplication.
A remainder is what is left over after grouping.
If a question asks 'how many full buses/boxes?' you IGNORE the remainder. If it asks 'how many buses needed?' you ROUND UP.
teach.example: 20 ÷ 6. How many sixes in 20? Three sixes is 18. We have 2 left. Answer: 3 remainder 2.

workedExample.question: 216 children, max 20 per class. What is the minimum number of classes needed?
steps: Those 16 still need a class, so we need one MORE.

practice.question: 468 children split into 9 teams. How many per team?
216 cupcakes put into boxes of 27. How many boxes?
practice.workingOut: 27 × 8 = 216. Answer: 8 boxes.

practice.question: A bus holds 52. 320 passengers need a ride. How many buses?
practice.workingOut: 320 ÷ 52 = 6 remainder 8. 6 buses leave 8 people behind, so 7 buses needed.

practice.question: 8 kg of rice into 500g bags. How many bags?
Tom saves £2.50 each week. How many weeks to save £40?

teach.title: Factors & multiples
teach.plain: Factors are numbers that divide evenly into another number. Multiples are the answers you get from times tables.
rules: A factor of 12 is a number that divides into 12 with NO remainder.
The factors of 12 are: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12.
Multiples of 5 are: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25... (the 5 times table).
Every number is a factor of itself and has 1 as a factor.
teach.example: Factors of 10: 1, 2, 5, 10 (because 1×10=10 and 2×5=10). Multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16, 20...

workedExample.question: List all the factors of 18.
steps: Try 4: 18 ÷ 4 has a remainder, so 4 is not a factor.
Try 5: 18 ÷ 5 has a remainder, so 5 is not a factor.
6 already on the list, so we stop.

practice.question: Which of these is a factor of 24?
practice.workingOut: 8 × 3 = 24, so 8 is a factor of 24.

practice.question: Which is NOT a multiple of 6?
practice.workingOut: 26 ÷ 6 = 4 remainder 2, so 26 is not a multiple of 6.

practice.question: How many factors does 10 have?
practice.workingOut: Factors of 10: 1, 2, 5, 10. That is 4 factors.

practice.question: What is the smallest number that is a multiple of both 3 and 4?
practice.workingOut: Multiples of 3: 3,6,9,12. Multiples of 4: 4,8,12. Smallest common one is 12.

practice.question: Which number has exactly 2 factors?
practice.workingOut: 7 has only 1 and 7 as factors. This makes it a prime number.

strands.name: Fractions, Decimals & Percentages
strands.description: Parts of a whole — three different ways of showing them.

teach.title: Fractions of amounts
teach.plain: A fraction tells you how many equal parts to split something into, and how many of those parts you want.
rules: The BOTTOM number (denominator) tells you how many equal parts.
The TOP number (numerator) tells you how many parts you want.
To find 3/5 of a number: divide by 5, then multiply by 3.
Divide first, then multiply. Bottom number first, top number second.

workedExample.question: What is 3/5 of 70?
steps: Divide the amount by the BOTTOM number: 70 ÷ 5 = 14.

teach.title: Equivalent fractions
teach.plain: Equivalent fractions are different ways of writing the same amount. 1/2 and 2/4 look different but mean the same.
rules: Multiply (or divide) the TOP and BOTTOM by the SAME number.
This makes a new fraction equal to the first.
To simplify: find the biggest number that divides into BOTH top and bottom.
Keep simplifying until no number (except 1) divides both.
teach.example: 6/8: divide top and bottom by 2 → 3/4. Cannot simplify further. So 6/8 = 3/4.

workedExample.question: Convert 5 and 6/8 into an improper fraction.
steps: An improper fraction has the top number bigger than the bottom.
Take the whole number (5) and multiply it by the denominator (8): 5 × 8 = 40.

practice.question: Which is equivalent to 1/2?
practice.workingOut: 3/6 → divide both by 3 → 1/2.

practice.question: Simplify 8/12.
practice.workingOut: Divide both by 4: 8÷4=2, 12÷4=3. Answer 2/3.

practice.question: 5 and 6/8 as an improper fraction?
Which fraction is equivalent to 4/6?
Simplify 10/15.
practice.workingOut: Divide both by 5: 10÷5=2, 15÷5=3. Answer 2/3.
Multiply top and bottom by 3: 3×3=9, 4×3=12. Answer 9.

teach.title: Decimals
teach.plain: Decimals are another way of writing fractions. The decimal point separates whole numbers from parts of a whole.
rules: First digit after the point = TENTHS (1/10).
Second digit after the point = HUNDREDTHS (1/100).
Third digit = THOUSANDTHS (1/1000).
To divide by 10, move the decimal point ONE place LEFT. By 100, TWO places. By 1000, THREE places.
teach.example: 0.7 means 7 tenths, or 7/10. 0.25 means 25 hundredths, or 25/100 = 1/4.

workedExample.question: What is 30,785 ÷ 1,000?
steps: Dividing by 1,000 means moving the decimal point THREE places LEFT.
30,785 has the decimal point at the end: 30,785. (or 30,785.0).

practice.workingOut: Move decimal 3 places left: 30.785.

practice.question: Which is the largest?
practice.workingOut: Compare tenths: 0.5, 0.5, 0.5, 0.4. First three tied at 5 tenths; then hundredths: 0.45 has 5, 0.50 has 0, 0.54 has 4. Largest is 0.54.
6 × 3 = 18. Two decimal places total, so 0.18.
Move decimal 1 place left: 0.25.

practice.question: What is 0.75 as a fraction in simplest form?

teach.title: Percentages
teach.plain: Per cent means 'out of 100'. 50% means 50 out of every 100, which is a half.
rules: 10% of anything = divide by 10.
50% = a half = divide by 2.
25% = a quarter = divide by 4.
1% = divide by 100.
To find other percentages, build them from the easy ones.

workedExample.question: Find 15% of £360.

practice.question: A coat costs £80, reduced by 25%. Sale price?
20 out of 80 pupils walk. What percentage?

strands.name: Measurement
strands.description: Money, time, length, weight and metric units.

teach.title: Money problems
teach.plain: Money uses pounds and pence. £1 = 100p. Write amounts with 2 decimal places (like £4.50, not £4.5).
rules: Line up the decimal points when you add or subtract money.
Always write money with 2 decimal places: £4.50, £0.05, £12.00.
To change pence to pounds, divide by 100 (move decimal 2 places left).
To change pounds to pence, multiply by 100.
teach.tryThis: If a pen is 45p and a rubber is 30p, how much for both?

workedExample.question: A bike costs £120. Jacob has saved £72.50. How much more does he need?
steps: Line up the decimal points: 120.00 minus 72.50.

practice.question: A £120 bike. Jacob saved £72.50. How much more needed?
2 pizzas cost £17. What is the cost of 5 pizzas?
Sam has £20. Buys 3 notebooks at £2.75 each. Change?
Tom has £2,500. Spends £875 on laptop, £345 on phone. How much left?
Mean of 4 amounts is £16. Three are £15, £9, £21. What is the 4th?
Emma has £450, saves £125, £150, £175 more. How much now?

teach.title: Time
teach.plain: Time uses base 60, not base 10! 60 minutes = 1 hour. The 24-hour clock goes 00:00 to 23:59.
rules: 60 seconds = 1 minute. 60 minutes = 1 hour. 24 hours = 1 day.
After midday, 1pm = 13:00, 2pm = 14:00... add 12 to pm times.
To find a time gap, count up to the next whole hour, then onwards.
Do NOT subtract 52 minutes from 25 minutes directly — borrow 60, not 100.
teach.tryThis: A film starts 14:45 and lasts 1 hour 35 minutes. What time does it end?

workedExample.question: From 08:52 to 17:23, how many minutes pass?

practice.question: From 08:52 to 17:23, how many minutes?
practice.workingOut: 8 min to 9:00, then 8 hours (480), then 23 min. Total: 511.

practice.question: A film starts 14:45, lasts 1hr 35min. End time?
Train leaves 09:35 arrives 11:12. Journey length?
practice.workingOut: 09:35 to 10:35 is 1 hour. 10:35 to 11:12 is 37 min. Total: 1hr 37min.

practice.question: How many minutes in 3 hours?
practice.workingOut: 3 × 60 = 180 minutes.

practice.question: 3pm using the 24-hour clock?
practice.workingOut: After midday, add 12: 3 + 12 = 15:00.

teach.title: Metric conversions
teach.plain: Metric units work in tens, hundreds and thousands — so converting means multiplying or dividing by 10, 100 or 1,000.
rules: To go from BIG to SMALL units (e.g. km to m), MULTIPLY.
To go from SMALL to BIG units (e.g. cm to m), DIVIDE.
teach.example: Convert 3.5 km to m: big to small, so × 1,000. 3.5 × 1,000 = 3,500 m.

workedExample.question: What is 3.75m - 68cm?
steps: Make the units the SAME first.

practice.question: How many metres in 3.5 km?
Convert 4,200 g to kg.
How many ml in 2.4 litres?
How many cm in 2.3 m?
A ribbon is 1.5 m long. Cut 40 cm off. How much left?

teach.title: Perimeter & area
teach.plain: Perimeter is the distance AROUND a shape. Area is the space INSIDE a shape.
rules: Perimeter: add up ALL the sides.
Area of a rectangle: length × width.
Perimeter is measured in cm, m, km (length units).
Area is measured in SQUARE units: cm², m².
teach.example: A rectangle 8 cm by 5 cm. Perimeter = 8+5+8+5 = 26 cm. Area = 8 × 5 = 40 cm².
teach.tryThis: Find the perimeter and area of a rectangle 10 cm × 4 cm.

workedExample.question: A rectangle is 12 cm long and 5 cm wide. Find its perimeter AND area.
steps: Perimeter = add all sides. A rectangle has two longs and two widths.

practice.question: A rectangle 12cm by 5cm. Perimeter?
Area of a rectangle 8cm by 6cm?
A square has area 64 cm². Length of one side?
practice.workingOut: 8 × 8 = 64, so side = 8 cm.

practice.question: Perimeter of a regular pentagon with one side 7 cm?
practice.workingOut: Regular pentagon has 5 equal sides. 5 × 7 = 35 cm.

practice.question: A garden is 20m by 15m. What is its perimeter?
Area of a square with sides 9 cm?

strands.name: Algebra Basics
strands.description: Missing numbers, simple equations and sequences.

teach.title: Missing numbers
teach.plain: In a missing-number problem, you work backwards. Use the OPPOSITE operation to the one in the question.
rules: + and - are opposites. × and ÷ are opposites.
Always CHECK by putting your answer back into the question.

workedExample.question: Solve 4a = 16.
steps: We need a number that, multiplied by 4, gives 16.

practice.question: What goes in the box? □ × 8 = 96
Solve 4a = 16.
A number minus 3,456 equals 5,432. What is the number?
Sum of two numbers is 10,000. One is 4,567. The other?

teach.title: Simple equations
teach.plain: An equation is a maths sentence with an equals sign. Letters stand for unknown numbers.
rules: The letter is a number we don't know yet.
Whatever you do to one side, do to the other.
To find the letter, undo the operation with its opposite.
2a means 2 × a. 3y means 3 × y.
teach.example: y + 5 = 12. Take 5 from both sides: y = 7.

workedExample.question: Expand 3(2y - 5).
steps: '3(2y - 5)' means 3 lots of everything inside the bracket.

practice.question: Simplify 3(2y - 5).
Solve 4a = 16. a = ?
If n + 7 = 15, what is n?
If 3x = 21, what is x?
What is the value of 2a + 3 when a = 5?

teach.title: Number sequences
teach.plain: A sequence is a list of numbers that follow a rule. Find the rule by looking at how the numbers change.
rules: Look at the difference between consecutive numbers.
If the gap is the same each time, the rule is ADD or SUBTRACT that number.
If the numbers double or triple, the rule is MULTIPLY.
Check the rule works for EVERY pair in the sequence.
teach.example: 3, 7, 11, 15, ... Gap is +4 each time. Next term: 15 + 4 = 19.

workedExample.question: What is the next number? 2, 6, 18, 54, ?
steps: Look at each gap: 2 to 6, 6 to 18, 18 to 54.
Is it adding? 6 - 2 = 4, 18 - 6 = 12, 54 - 18 = 36. The differences change, so NOT adding a constant.

practice.question: Next number? 3, 7, 11, 15, ___
practice.workingOut: Add 4 each time. 15 + 4 = 19.

practice.question: Next number? 2, 6, 18, 54, ___
practice.workingOut: Multiply by 3 each time. 54 × 3 = 162.

practice.question: Next number? 100, 87, 74, 61, ___
practice.workingOut: Subtract 13 each time. 61 - 13 = 48.

practice.question: Next number? 1, 4, 9, 16, ___
practice.workingOut: Square numbers: 1², 2², 3², 4², 5² = 25.

practice.question: Next number? 64, 32, 16, 8, ___
practice.workingOut: Halve each time. 8 ÷ 2 = 4.

strands.name: Geometry & Shape
strands.description: Name and describe 2D shapes, triangles, quadrilaterals, angles and lines of symmetry.

teach.title: Sides and Corners
teach.plain: Flat shapes are made of straight sides and corners. The corners where two sides meet are called vertices.
rules: A side is one straight edge of the shape.
A vertex is a corner where two sides meet, and vertices means more than one corner.
A shape always has the same number of sides as vertices.
teach.example: A pentagon has 5 sides and 5 vertices, and a hexagon has 6 sides and 6 vertices.
teach.tryThis: How many vertices does an octagon have if it has 8 sides?
teach.tryAnswer: 8 vertices, because a shape has the same number of vertices as sides.

workedExample.question: A shape has 4 sides that are all the same length. How many vertices does it have?
steps: Count the sides described: the shape has 4 sides.
Remember a shape has the same number of vertices as sides.

practice.question: How many sides does a hexagon have?
practice.workingOut: A hexagon always has 6 straight sides.

practice.question: A shape has 5 vertices. How many sides does it have?
practice.workingOut: A shape has the same number of sides as vertices, so 5 vertices means 5 sides.

practice.question: Which word means the corners of a shape?
practice.workingOut: Vertices is the word for the corners where two sides meet.

practice.question: A pentagon and a hexagon are placed side by side. How many sides do they have altogether?
practice.workingOut: A pentagon has 5 sides and a hexagon has 6 sides, and 5 plus 6 is 11.

practice.question: An octagon has 8 sides. How many more sides does it have than a pentagon?
practice.workingOut: A pentagon has 5 sides, and 8 minus 5 is 3.

practice.question: A shape has all sides equal and 3 vertices. What is it?
practice.workingOut: A shape with 3 vertices has 3 sides, which makes it a triangle.

teach.title: Naming Shapes
teach.plain: Triangles have 3 sides and quadrilaterals have 4 sides. We give them special names based on their sides and angles.
rules: A square has 4 equal sides and 4 right angles, and a rectangle has 2 pairs of equal sides and 4 right angles.
A rhombus has 4 equal sides but is pushed over, so its corners are not right angles.
An equilateral triangle has 3 equal sides, an isosceles triangle has 2 equal sides, and a right-angled triangle has one square corner.
teach.example: A shape with 4 equal sides and 4 square corners is a square, while a shape with 4 equal sides and no square corners is a rhombus.
teach.tryThis: A triangle has two sides the same length. What is it called?
teach.tryAnswer: An isosceles triangle, because it has exactly two equal sides.

workedExample.question: A four-sided shape has two long sides the same and two short sides the same, with four right angles. What is it?
steps: It has 4 sides, so it is a quadrilateral.
It has two pairs of equal sides, not all four equal.
It has four right angles, which matches a rectangle.

practice.question: How many sides does a quadrilateral have?
practice.workingOut: A quadrilateral always has 4 sides.

practice.question: A triangle has all three sides the same length. What is it called?
practice.workingOut: Three equal sides means it is an equilateral triangle.

practice.question: Which shape has 4 equal sides but no right angles?
practice.workingOut: A rhombus has 4 equal sides but is leaning over, so it has no right angles.

practice.question: A triangle has one square corner. What is it called?
practice.workingOut: A square corner is a right angle, so it is a right-angled triangle.

practice.question: Which shape has 4 equal sides AND 4 right angles?
practice.workingOut: A square has 4 equal sides and 4 right angles.

practice.question: A triangle has exactly two sides the same length. What is it called?
practice.workingOut: Two equal sides means it is an isosceles triangle.

teach.title: Angles and Mirror Lines
teach.plain: An angle tells us how much a corner opens. A line of symmetry is a fold line where both halves match exactly.
rules: A right angle is a perfect square corner, an acute angle is smaller than a right angle, and an obtuse angle is bigger than a right angle but still open.
The wider a corner opens, the bigger the angle.
A line of symmetry splits a shape into two halves that are mirror images of each other.
teach.example: The corner of a book cover is a right angle, the narrow point of a slice of pizza is an acute angle, and the wide open corner of a slimline ramp is an obtuse angle.
teach.tryThis: Is an angle that is smaller than a square corner acute or obtuse?
teach.tryAnswer: Acute, because acute angles are smaller than a right angle.

workedExample.question: A square is folded so the left half lands exactly on the right half. How many lines of symmetry does a square have in total?
steps: A square can fold down the middle from top to bottom and from side to side.
It can also fold along both diagonals from corner to corner.
That gives 2 straight folds plus 2 diagonal folds.

practice.question: An angle that is smaller than a right angle is called what?
practice.workingOut: Acute angles are smaller than a right angle.

practice.question: An angle that is bigger than a right angle but still open is called what?
practice.workingOut: Obtuse angles are bigger than a right angle.

practice.question: A perfect square corner is which kind of angle?
practice.workingOut: A square corner is exactly a right angle.

practice.question: Which angle is the biggest?
practice.workingOut: An obtuse angle is bigger than both an acute angle and a right angle.

practice.question: How many lines of symmetry does a square have?
practice.workingOut: A square folds in half four ways, so it has 4 lines of symmetry.

practice.question: How many lines of symmetry does a rectangle that is not a square have?
practice.workingOut: A rectangle folds in half top to bottom and side to side, giving 2 lines of symmetry.

strands.name: Position & Direction
strands.description: Read and write coordinates in the first quadrant, describe position and translate points and shapes.

teach.title: Reading Points (x, y)
teach.plain: A coordinate gives the position of a point using two numbers in brackets. The first number tells you how far across, and the second tells you how far up.
rules: Always read across first, then up, so the order is (x, y).
The first number counts steps to the right from the corner, and the second number counts steps up.
A handy reminder is along the corridor and then up the stairs.
teach.example: The point (3, 2) means start at the corner, move 3 steps right, then 2 steps up.
teach.tryThis: For the point (5, 1), how many steps do you go up?
teach.tryAnswer: 1 step up, because the second number tells you how far up to go.

workedExample.question: A point is 4 steps across and 6 steps up. What are its coordinates?
steps: Write the across number first, which is 4.
Write the up number second, which is 6.
Put them in brackets in the order (across, up).

practice.question: In the coordinate (7, 2), what does the 7 tell you?
practice.workingOut: The first number tells you how far across to move.

practice.question: A point is 2 steps across and 5 steps up. What are its coordinates?
practice.workingOut: Across comes first, so it is (2, 5).

practice.question: Which number do you always read first in a coordinate?
practice.workingOut: You always read the across number (x) first.

practice.question: For the point (3, 0), how many steps up do you go?
practice.workingOut: The second number is 0, so you go no steps up.

practice.question: A point is 6 across and 6 up. What are its coordinates?
practice.workingOut: 6 across then 6 up is written as (6, 6).

practice.question: In the coordinate (4, 9), what does the 9 tell you?
practice.workingOut: The second number tells you how far up to move.

teach.title: Which Point is Where
teach.plain: When points are described by their coordinates, you can work out which one is highest, furthest across or in a special place.
rules: Compare the first numbers to see which point is further across.
Compare the second numbers to see which point is higher up.
A point with both numbers the same, like (4, 4), sits on the diagonal line going up from the corner.
teach.example: Point A is at (2, 5) and point B is at (6, 1). Point B is further across, but point A is higher up.
teach.tryThis: Point P is at (3, 7) and point Q is at (3, 2). Which point is higher up?
teach.tryAnswer: Point P, because 7 is bigger than 2 so it is higher up.

workedExample.question: Point A is at (1, 8) and point B is at (5, 8). Which point is further to the right?
steps: Both points have the same up number, which is 8.
Compare the across numbers: A is 1 and B is 5.
5 is bigger than 1, so B is further to the right.

practice.question: Point A is at (2, 3) and point B is at (8, 3). Which is further across?
practice.workingOut: B has across number 8, which is bigger than A's 2, so B is further across.

practice.question: Point C is at (4, 1) and point D is at (4, 9). Which is higher up?
practice.workingOut: D has up number 9, which is bigger than C's 1, so D is higher.

practice.question: Three points are at (1, 1), (1, 5) and (1, 3). Which is the highest?
practice.workingOut: The up numbers are 1, 5 and 3, and 5 is the biggest, so (1, 5) is highest.

practice.question: A point sits on the diagonal where the across and up numbers are equal. Which point is it?
practice.workingOut: (5, 5) has equal across and up numbers, so it sits on the diagonal.

practice.question: Point E is at (0, 4). Where is it?
practice.workingOut: The first number 0 is across and the second number 4 is up, so 0 across and 4 up.

practice.question: Points are at (3, 2), (7, 2) and (5, 2). Which is furthest to the left?
practice.workingOut: The across numbers are 3, 7 and 5, and 3 is smallest, so (3, 2) is furthest left.

teach.title: Sliding Points
teach.plain: A translation slides a point or shape left, right, up or down without turning it. You just change the coordinates.
rules: Moving right adds to the first number and moving left takes away from the first number.
Moving up adds to the second number and moving down takes away from the second number.
The shape stays exactly the same size and the same way round, it only slides.
teach.example: The point (2, 3) moved 4 steps right becomes (6, 3), because 2 plus 4 is 6 and the up number stays the same.
teach.tryThis: Move the point (5, 5) up by 3. What are the new coordinates?
teach.tryAnswer: (5, 8), because moving up adds 3 to the second number, and 5 plus 3 is 8.

workedExample.question: The point (3, 4) is moved 2 steps right and 1 step up. What are its new coordinates?
steps: Moving 2 right adds 2 to the first number: 3 plus 2 is 5.
Moving 1 up adds 1 to the second number: 4 plus 1 is 5.
Put the new numbers together as (5, 5).

practice.question: Move the point (2, 2) by 3 steps right. What are the new coordinates?
practice.workingOut: Moving right adds 3 to the first number, so 2 plus 3 is 5, giving (5, 2).

practice.question: Move the point (6, 4) by 2 steps down. What are the new coordinates?
practice.workingOut: Moving down takes 2 from the second number, so 4 minus 2 is 2, giving (6, 2).

practice.question: The point (1, 1) moves 4 up. Where is it now?
practice.workingOut: Moving up adds 4 to the second number, so 1 plus 4 is 5, giving (1, 5).

practice.question: When a shape is translated, what happens to its size?
practice.workingOut: A translation only slides the shape, so the size stays exactly the same.

practice.question: Move the point (7, 5) by 3 steps left. What are the new coordinates?
practice.workingOut: Moving left takes 3 from the first number, so 7 minus 3 is 4, giving (4, 5).

practice.question: The point (3, 3) moves 2 right and 2 up. Where is it now?
practice.workingOut: 2 right makes 3 plus 2 is 5, and 2 up makes 3 plus 2 is 5, giving (5, 5).

strands.name: Statistics
strands.description: Read and compare data from real bar charts, pictograms and tables.

teach.title: Bars Show Amounts
teach.plain: A bar chart uses bars to show how many of each thing there are. The taller the bar, the bigger the amount. You read up from the top of a bar to the number on the side.
rules: Each bar stands for one group, like cats or dogs.
Read up from the top of the bar across to the number scale on the left to find its value.
To compare two bars, find the difference by taking the smaller number away from the bigger one.
teach.example: If the cats bar reaches 8 and the dogs bar reaches 5, there are 3 more cats than dogs because 8 minus 5 is 3.
teach.tryThis: If a bar reaches halfway between 4 and 6, what number is it?

workedExample.question: Look at the bar chart. How many more white cars are there than blue cars?
steps: Find the White bar and read up to the top: it reaches 9.
Find the Blue bar: it reaches 4.
Subtract to find the difference: 9 minus 4 is 5.

practice.question: Look at the bar chart. How many cats are there?
practice.workingOut: Read up from the top of the Cats bar to 8.

practice.question: Look at the bar chart. How many more cats are there than rabbits?
practice.workingOut: The Cats bar is 8 and the Rabbits bar is 3, and 8 minus 3 is 5.

practice.question: Look at the bar chart. Which sport has the tallest bar?
practice.workingOut: The Tennis bar reaches 9, the highest of the three.

practice.question: Look at the bar chart. How many balls are there altogether?
practice.workingOut: Add the bars: 6 plus 9 plus 4 is 19.

practice.question: Look at the bar chart. How many fewer rainy days were there than sunny days?
practice.workingOut: Sunny is 10 and Rainy is 5, and 10 minus 5 is 5.

practice.question: Look at the bar chart. Which two bars are the same height?
practice.workingOut: Both the Buses and Trains bars reach 4, so they are the same height.

teach.title: One Symbol Stands for Many
teach.plain: A pictogram uses a picture to stand for a number of things. You count the symbols in a row and times them by what one symbol is worth.
rules: Check the key first to see how many one symbol stands for.
Count the symbols in the row, then multiply by the value of one symbol.
More symbols means a bigger amount.
teach.example: If one star stands for 2 books and a row has 3 stars, that is 6 books, because 3 times 2 is 6.
teach.tryThis: One apple stands for 5 apples. A row has 4 apples. How many is that?

workedExample.question: Look at the pictogram. How many children are in Class A?
steps: Read the key: one face stands for 10 children.
Count the faces in Class A's row: there are 5.

practice.question: Look at the pictogram. How many sweets does Amir have?
practice.workingOut: Amir has 3 stars and each star is 5 sweets, so 3 times 5 is 15.

practice.question: Look at the pictogram. How many red cars are there?
practice.workingOut: Red has 2 cars and each stands for 10, so 2 times 10 is 20.

practice.question: Look at the pictogram. How many coins are in Jar A?
practice.workingOut: Jar A has 5 coins and each is worth 4, so 5 times 4 is 20.

practice.question: Look at the pictogram. How many trees are in the Park?
practice.workingOut: The Park has 3 trees and each stands for 10, so 3 times 10 is 30.

practice.question: Look at the pictogram. How many more votes did Cats get than Dogs?
practice.workingOut: Cats is 6 times 2 is 12 votes, Dogs is 4 times 2 is 8 votes, and 12 minus 8 is 4.

practice.question: Look at the pictogram. How many books were read on Tuesday?
practice.workingOut: Tuesday has 5 books and each is worth 2, so 5 times 2 is 10.

teach.title: Reading Tables
teach.plain: A table organises numbers in rows so you can find totals, differences and the most or least. You find the right row and read across to the number.
rules: Find the row you need and read across to the number.
Add numbers together to find a total.
The biggest number is the most and the smallest number is the least.
teach.example: If a table shows Monday 12, Tuesday 8 and Wednesday 15 visitors, Wednesday had the most because 15 is the biggest.
teach.tryThis: A table shows 5 red, 9 blue and 2 green. Which has the least?
teach.tryAnswer: Green, because 2 is the smallest number.

workedExample.question: Look at the table. How many visitors came over the three days altogether?
steps: Add the first two: 12 plus 8 is 20.
Add the last one: 20 plus 15 is 35.

practice.question: Look at the table. Which colour has the most counters?
practice.workingOut: Blue has 9, the biggest number, so it has the most.

practice.question: Look at the table. How many counters are there altogether?
practice.workingOut: Add the rows: 5 plus 9 plus 2 is 16.

practice.question: Look at the table. How many more visitors came on Wednesday than Tuesday?
practice.workingOut: Wednesday is 15 and Tuesday is 8, and 15 minus 8 is 7.

practice.question: Look at the table. How many cakes were sold over the two days?
practice.workingOut: Add the rows: 6 plus 11 is 17.

practice.question: Look at the table. Which class has the fewest pupils?
practice.workingOut: Class A has 24, the smallest number, so it has the fewest.

practice.question: Look at the table. What is the difference between the two boxes?
practice.workingOut: Box 1 is 10 and Box 2 is 4, and 10 minus 4 is 6.
